---
title: "Getting Started with Shiny Modules"
description: "A practical walkthrough of how Shiny modules helped me tame a 3,000-line server function and actually made my app faster to build."
author: "Andrew Fuller"
date: "2026-05-15"
categories: [R, Shiny, Tutorial]
image: "thumbnail.png"
toc: true
---

## The Problem

Anyone who has built a non-trivial Shiny app has probably ended up staring at a `server.R` that grew to an unmanageable size. Mine hit around 3,000 lines before I finally caved and learned how modules work properly.

The app was a clinical dashboard for rehabilitation benchmarking — multiple tabs, multiple filters, reactive chains that crossed each other in ways that were hard to reason about. Every time I needed to change how one filter worked, I'd spend 20 minutes tracking down all the places it was referenced.

## What Are Shiny Modules?

A Shiny module is a pair of functions — a **UI function** and a **server function** — that encapsulate a piece of your app. The key insight is that modules have their own **namespace**: reactive values, inputs, and outputs inside a module can't accidentally clash with each other.

```r
# A simple module UI function
myModuleUI <- function(id) {
  ns <- NS(id)   # <-- creates the namespace
  tagList(
    selectInput(ns("my_select"), "Choose:", choices = c("A", "B", "C")),
    tableOutput(ns("my_table"))
  )
}

# The paired server function
myModuleServer <- function(id, data) {
  moduleServer(id, function(input, output, session) {
    filtered <- reactive({
      data |> dplyr::filter(group == input$my_select)
    })

    output$my_table <- renderTable(filtered())
  })
}
```

And then in your main app:

```r
# ui.R
ui <- fluidPage(
  myModuleUI("tab1"),
  myModuleUI("tab2")   # completely isolated from tab1
)

# server.R
server <- function(input, output, session) {
  myModuleServer("tab1", data = my_data)
  myModuleServer("tab2", data = my_other_data)
}
```

The `NS(id)` call is the magic — it prefixes every `inputId` and `outputId` with the module's id, so `"my_select"` inside `"tab1"` becomes `"tab1-my_select"` in the DOM.

## Passing Reactives Between Modules

The gotcha that tripped me up initially: **reactives are passed as functions, not values**.

```r
# WRONG — passes the current value, not a reactive expression
myModuleServer("mod", data = my_reactive_data())

# RIGHT — passes the reactive itself
myModuleServer("mod", data = my_reactive_data)
```

Inside the module, you then call `data()` where you need the value. This means your module stays in sync automatically when the upstream reactive changes.

## The Result

After the refactor:

- `server.R` went from ~3,000 lines to ~120 lines
- Each module lives in its own file, making navigation trivial
- I can test modules in isolation
- Adding a new tab is now a 20-minute job instead of an afternoon

If you're building anything beyond a demo app and haven't tried modules yet, start now. Your future self will thank you.

---

*Questions or corrections? Open an issue on [GitHub](https://github.com/andrew-fuller).*
